content.full_text:
August 22, 1977

Mrs. John Laufman
18591 Ravenwood Drive
Saratoga, California 95070

Dear Mrs. Laufman:

This letter responds to your request for advice regarding reporting obligations for the Family Women of America (FWA). You described the FWA as an organization created to promote the interests of the American family and home. Further, you stated that the FWA does not intend to endorse candidates, but the FWA does distribute "Voters' Guides" by mail and by hand delivery. You indicated that the "Voters' Guides" will be distributed at various times throughout the year, and the FWA intends to substantially increase the distributions during election periods. You provided two examples of past "Voters' Guides" and asked if either or both of the examples would result in reporting obligations under the Political Reform Act.

The Political Reform Act contains two sets of provisions which are relevant to your request for advice - campaign disclosure provisions and lobbying disclosure provisions. Based on information you have provided, we have concluded that the FWA has no reporting obligations under the campaign disclosure provisions of the Act, but may have reporting obligations under the lobbying provisions of the Act. The reasons for our conclusions are explained in the following analysis.

The campaign disclosure provisions of the Political Reform Act provide that an organization such as the FWA which raises or spends $500 or more in a calendar year for "political purposes" becomes a "committee," and must file campaign disclosure statements (see Government Code Sections 82013, 82015 and 82025). Regulations adopted by the Fair Political Practices Commission define "political purposes" to mean "...made for the purpose of influencing or attempting to influence the action of voters for or against the nomination or election of a candidate or candidates or the qualification or passage of any measure.(see 2 Cal. Administrative Code Sections 18215 and 18225).

John Laufman                           -2-                    August 22, 1977


To determine if the FWA has reporting obligations under the
campaign disclosure provisions of the Act, the initial question
which must be answered is whether expenditures by the FWA for
"Voters' Guides" are made for "political purposes." 2 Cal. Adm.
Code Section 18225 states that an expenditure for political pur-
poses "...includes any monetary or non-monetary payment made by
any person...that is used for communications which expressly advo-
cate the nomination, election or defeat of a clearly identified
candidate or candidates, or the qualification, passage or defeat
of a clearly identified ballot measure." This regulation further
states: "A communication 'expressly advocates' the nomination,
election or defeat of a candidate or the qualification, passage
or defeat of a measure, if it contains express words of advocacy
such as 'for', 'elect', 'support', 'cast your ballot', 'vote
against', 'defeat', 'reject', 'sign petitions for', or otherwise
rate to a clearly identified candidate or measure so that the
communication, taken as a whole, unambiguously urges a particular
result in an election." (Emphasis added.)

After analyzing the two examples of past "Voters' Guides"
which you provided, the staff of the Commission has determined
that these examples do not unambiguously urge a particular result
in an election. Consequently, contributions received and expendi-
tures made in connection with producing and distributing the
"Voters' Guides" are not reportable under the campaign disclosure
provisions of the Act.

However, you should keep in mind that a modification of the
content of the "Voters' Guides" could make them communications
which unambiguously urge a particular result in an election. For
example, the 1976 "Voters' Guide" identified the voting records
of selected legislators with respect to selected legislation. In
addition, some of the legislators were identified as incumbents
running in the June 8th election. If this "Voters' Guide" had
characterized any of the legislator's voting records as either
"good" or "bad," or if the selection of the legislators or legis-
lation had been done in such a way as to make clear that the FWA
supported or opposed any candidates, then the communication would
have been made for "political purposes" and expenditures made in
connection with the communication would have been reportable under
the Act, assuming the FWA met the monetary threshold for being
a "committee" pursuant to Government Code Section 82013. However,
the expenditures could be exempted from the reporting requirements
under 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18225(c)(4)(i) if the communica-
tion is a regularly published newsletter "...whose circulation is
limited to an organization's members, employees, shareholders,
other affiliated individuals and those who request or purchase
the publication. This paragraph applies only to the costs regu-
larly incurred in publishing and distributing the newsletter or
periodical. If additional costs are incurred because the newslet-
ter or periodical is issued on other than its regular schedule,
expanded in circulation, or substantially altered in style, size
or format, the additional costs are expenditures." (Emphasis added.)

John Laufm                    -3-                    August 22, 1977


     With regard to the lobbying provisions of the Act, the FWA
may incur reporting obligations as a result of expenditures made
to produce and distribute "Voters' Guides". Government Code
Section 86108(b) requires a disclosure statement (Form 650) to be
filed in the month following any month in which two hundred and
fifty dollars ($250) or more was spent to influence state legisla-
tive or state administrative action. Payments to influence legis-
lative or administrative action are defined by Government Code
Section 82045 and include, but are not limited to, payments in
connection with soliciting or urging other persons to enter into
direct communication with any elective state official, legislative
official, or agency official.

     After analyzing the two examples of "Voters' Guides" provided
by you, the staff of the Commission has determined that the 1976
"Voters' Guide" does not attempt to influence legislative or admin-
istrative action, and that expenditures made in connection with
this "Voters' Guide" are, therefore, not reportable under the Act.
However, it was concluded that the January-March 1977 "Voters'
Guide" does attempt to influence legislative action. This Voters'
Guide" attempts to influence legislative action in that it requests
the reader to call or write legislators after noting a series of
"Questions To Consider" which suggest policy positions of the FWA.
Therefore, if $250 or more is spent in a calendar month to produce
and distribute a "Voters' Guide" similar to the January-March 1977
"Voters' Guide" the FWA should file a Form 650 with the Secretary
of State in the month following the month in which $250 or more
was spent.

     Enclosed you will find copies of the various documents which
have been cited in this letter. I hope that these enclosures and
the above advice are helpful, and if you have additional questions
or if I may be of further assistance, please do not hesitate to
contact me.

                                        Sincerely,


                                        John C. Greenwood
                                        Program Coordinator

JCG:bw

Enclosures

May 25, 1977

Fair Political Practices Commission
1100 "F" Street Building
Post Office Box 207
Sacramento, Ca. 95814

Dear Sirs,

Please examine the enclosed material
and notify me as to the legality of our or-
ganization's activities. This type of information
at election time. We would like to circulate
the Voter's Guide by mail and at the Precinct
level.

We do not intend to endorse any candidate.
We simply want to circulate the voting record
of the people who represent us in Sacramento
and Washington, D.C.

We are planning to register with the
Internal Revenue Service as a non-profit ed-
ucational organization.

Please advise us on how the F.P.P. law
will affect our project.

Thank you for your attention and help
in this matter.

Sincerely,

Mrs. John Laufman
18501 Ravenwood Dr.
Saratoga, Ca. 95070

FAMILY WOMEN OF AMERICA

This Nation Under God in the Judeo-Christian Heritage


Dear Friend,

Family Women of America was formed on July 4th, 1975. We are
an organization of women from many different backgrounds and
with diverse family situations. We have a common goal to
provide a voice for the American family. We believe the
continuing strength of our nation depends upon the solidity
of its family structure. However, the family is not adequately represented in our society, especially in the areas
of legislation and education. For this reason, we have joined
together in a common effort toward preserving the central
importance of the family in America, and most particularly
in Santa Clara County.

Our goals are three-fold:

To: PRESERVE THE AMERICAN FAMILY AND HOME
    PROMOTE RESPECT FOR HUMAN LIFE
    PROMOTE HIGH MORAL STANDARDS IN EDUCATION,
    GOVERNMENT AND COMMUNITY LIFE

At present we are functioning mainly as an information and
referral service. Our activities as individuals are concentrated on educating and motivating the public through use of
the media -- radio, television and newspapers.

Family Women of America holds monthly meetings, limited at
present to the Board of Directors, and issues a monthly
newsletter. The first copy of this newsletter is complementary to interested individuals. We appreciate and welcome
all inquiries, and we answer them as promptly and completely
as possible. Enclosed is a card on which you can indicate
your special area of interest. Please fill it out and mail
it back to us so we can assist you in gaining more information.

Thank you for your interest.

Sincerely,

Peggy Beaudoin

Peggy Beaudoin, Secretary
FAMILY WOMEN OF AMERICA
